titleStmt.title: Letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery to William Coote, 27 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery (1844-1924) to William Coote. Originally a Liberal and a strong supporter of Gladstone, Montgomery was also a firm Unionist, but by 1916 he believed that Ulster Unionists had no choice but to accept Lloyd George's proposal for a six-county Northern Ireland. William Coote (1853-1924) was a politician and in 1916 he was representing South Tyrone as a Unionist MP and continued to do so until 1922. The letter talks about the choices that Edward Carson will have to make for the good of the Unionist movement. Montgomery also writes to Coote to enclose various letters that are not included with this copy.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Private
Copy 27th May, 1916. Dear Mr. Coote, I enclose a copy of the draft of a letter I intended
sending to the "Times", but which I have now entrusted to
a safe hand in London to use or not as he thinks wise. Knowing what very good information and advice
Mr. Asquith got from many people of influence and ability
over here, I am disappointed at the character of his statement .
The position is very difficult. Sir Edward Carson woud be
serving our interests badly by putting himself too violently
in conflict with any strong current of public opinion in
England in favour of some sort of a "settlement." I am
sure he will not give away our position here in substance,
but he may have to take up a position which, to some of
our more stiff-necked friends here, will look like some
sort of surrender; and it will be necessary, above all
things, to prevent our people losing confidence in him,
as, if they do, we are lost. We have no other friend of
sufficiently commanding ability and position in the
political world to steer us safely through the very difficult
water ahead. I am rather pleased to find how nearly the wording
of the second resolution I send you corresponds with what
Sir Edward Carson said in response to Mr. Asquith's statement. Yours very truly,
P.S. I enclose a typed copy of the resolutions I drafted
at your suggestion, and I add a copy of a further resolution
which I think we might very well pass. I think it would be
better that these resolutions should not be proposed and
seconded by me , but by some of the rank and file of the
Delegates. I enclose a copy of a letter I am sending to each of
the members of the conference on Tuesday in hopes that it
will save time at the meeting.
